findings.message: The ADR limits the new `task-contract.json` validation to referential integrity, but the pre-review gate only checks entries that a task declares. That leaves an unattributed catalogue entry invisible to the new gate, so a Red/Yellow `impl_catalog` signal can still be deferred to merge-gate, contradicting the stated shift-left guarantee for contract violations. The plan needs a completeness invariant for relevant catalogue entries, not just RI of declared entries.

The ADR includes shape-only stubs in the stated shift-left problem, saying unimplemented code with only the shape in place should be caught earlier, but D3/D5 later define the new gate as impl_catalog blue only and explicitly state correct-signature stubs pass because body/liveness is reviewer-owned. That makes the plan internally inconsistent about the gate's guarantee and can lead implementation to expect body-aware blocking that the decision forbids. Remove stubs from the shift-left target/benefit, or change the decision to include a body-aware check.

The ADR frontmatter records every decision D1-D5 as `status: accepted`, but the README index adds this ADR as `Proposed`. That makes the plan artifacts contradict each other about whether this baseline ADR is accepted; update the index status to match the ADR's accepted decisions.

T002's orphan-detection step is backwards: it only walks `ContractedEntryRef`s already present in `task-contract.json`, so it cannot detect scope entries that were never attributed. `IN-03`/`AC-02` require comparing the contract against the full in-scope catalogue entry set, so as written this step cannot satisfy the orphan-entry case (and T007(b) is impossible to implement literally).

AC-05 is marked covered by T002/T007, but those tasks only create and test the conformance summary text; the implementation plan routes successful checks to stdout and never adds a task that appends the verified-conformance line to the reviewer briefing required by IN-06/AC-05. As written, the plan can complete with no briefing update, so reviewers would not receive the promised scope-of-guarantee notice.

This catalogue says the gate verifies all catalogue entries contracted to the track, while the spec and OQ-1 resolution define the gate as review-scope-specific via `cmd.group` and `<group>-type-signals.json`. If implemented from this contract, one scope review could be blocked by unrelated layers, defeating the per-scope pre-review gate design.

The plan makes `--group` do double duty as both the review scope and a TDDD layer: OQ-1 says the gate reads the TypeSignalsDocument for `cmd.group`, filters `ContractedEntryRef.layer == cmd.group`, and T008 then prepends that same gate to every `track-local-review-fix-codex` invocation. But the type catalogue models `group` as `ReviewGroupName` while the signal reader needs a `LayerId`, and review groups include non-layer scopes such as `plan-artifacts`. With the current universal wiring, those review runs will fail closed before the reviewer can start. The plan needs an explicit review-scope-to-layer mapping or a defined bypass for non-layer scopes before T008 is executable.

`task_contract_check` is specified to build the filesystem adapters from the CLI `items_dir` and call the use case directly, while the same plan/catalogue makes `TaskContractDriver` the primary adapter responsible for parsing `TaskContractInput` and rendering `PreReviewGateOutcome` to `CommandOutcome`. That leaves two conflicting execution paths: using `task_contract_driver()` ignores the `--items-dir` value because it wires a default path, while using `task_contract_check()` bypasses the primary adapter and duplicates driver responsibilities in the composition root. The plan needs one coherent path, e.g. construct the driver with adapters built from the requested `items_dir` and dispatch `TaskContractInput::Check`, or remove `items_dir` from the driver input and keep rendering outside it.

The spec and implementation plan disagree on the workflow command being gated: IN-05/AC-03/AC-06 require the gate before/blocking `sotp review local`, while T008 wires only `bin/sotp review fix-local` via `track-local-review-fix-codex`. Because T008 is marked as satisfying those criteria, the artifacts can claim AC-06 coverage while implementing a different review invocation path. Align the spec criteria with `review fix-local` or change T008 to gate the command named by the spec.

The dependency narrative lets T005 run before the composition root it calls exists. T005 explicitly constructs `TaskContractCompositionRoot` and calls `task_contract_check`, but the section says T005 depends only on T004 while T006 is the task that adds `TaskContractCompositionRoot`. A task runner following this plan can schedule/assign T005 before T006 and produce an unbuildable CLI task; add a T005 dependency on T006 or reorder the composition task before the CLI execute task.

`ImplCatalogSignalReaderPort` is traced to `spec.json#IN-02`, but IN-02 is the impl-planner responsibility to author `task-contract.json`. This port reads existing per-layer impl_catalog signal documents, so the traceability should point to the gate/signal-join requirement such as IN-04/CN-05. The same mismatch appears on `FsImplCatalogSignalReader`, making the catalogue claim the signal-reader work satisfies the wrong spec element.

OS-01 says the gate input is rustdoc JSON, but IN-04/CN-05 and the Phase 2/3 artifacts define the gate as reading existing impl_catalog/type-signals documents and explicitly not changing SignalEvaluatorV2. That gives implementers two incompatible input contracts; following OS-01 would push the design toward re-reading rustdoc instead of using the signal table, violating the intended signal-reuse boundary.
